title: TypeScript Advanced Types
summary: Conditional types, mapped types, template literal types, and the `infer` keyword — the type-level programming layer of TypeScript.

questions.prompt: What does `T extends U ? A : B` mean in TypeScript?
choices: T inherits from U at runtime
If T is assignable to U, resolve to type A; otherwise resolve to type B
T and U are merged unless both are B
A compile error if T is not a class
questions.explanation: Conditional types are ternary expressions at the type level. `extends` here means 'is assignable to' — not class inheritance. The entire expression is resolved at compile time and produces either type A or type B.

questions.prompt: What is `Result`?
questions.code:
type OnlyStrings<T> = T extends string ? T : never;
type Result = OnlyStrings<string | number | boolean>;
choices: Error — conditional types can't use unions
questions.explanation: When T is a union, conditional types distribute over each member. `string extends string → string`, `number extends string → never`, `boolean extends string → never`. Union: `string | never | never = string`.

questions.prompt: What does `infer U` do inside a conditional type?
choices: Declares U as a type alias
Captures the matched portion of a type pattern and binds it to U for use in the true branch
Checks if U exists at runtime
questions.explanation: `infer` is a type-level pattern match capture. `T extends (infer U)[]` says: if T is an array, capture the element type as U. You can then use U in the true branch. Zero runtime impact.

questions.prompt: What is `E`?
questions.code:
type ElementType<T> = T extends (infer U)[] ? U : never;
type E = ElementType<string[]>;
questions.explanation: `string[]` matches the pattern `(infer U)[]` with U = `string`. The conditional is true, so the result is U = `string`.

questions.prompt: A mapped type `{ [K in keyof T]: Promise<T[K]> }` applied to `{ name: string; age: number }` produces:
questions.explanation: The mapped type iterates over every key K of T. For each, the value becomes `Promise<T[K]>`. So `name` (string) becomes `Promise<string>` and `age` (number) becomes `Promise<number>`.

questions.prompt: `Partial<T>` is implemented internally as:
choices: A mapped type: `{ [K in keyof T]?: T[K] }`
A built-in compiler primitive with no type definition
questions.explanation: `Partial<T>` is a mapped type that adds `?` to every property. You can see this in TypeScript's `lib.es5.d.ts`. Understanding this lets you write your own custom variants like `DeepPartial`.

questions.prompt: What type is `Handler`?
choices: Error — template literals can't use unions
questions.explanation: Template literal types distribute over union members. `Capitalize<'click'>` = `'Click'`, so `` `on${...}` `` gives `'onClick'`. Same for `'focus'` → `'onFocus'`. Result: `'onClick' | 'onFocus'`.

questions.prompt: What is `never` used for in an exhaustive switch default?
choices: As an assertion: if TypeScript allows reaching this branch, all switch cases are NOT exhaustive — a new union member was added but not handled
To mark a function as abstract
To disable TypeScript checking in that branch
questions.explanation: Passing a value to `function exhaustive(x: never): never` only compiles if `x` IS never. If you add a new union member and don't handle it in the switch, the default branch receives a value of that type (not never) — TypeScript errors. It's a compile-time completeness check.

questions.prompt: What is `Wrapped`?
questions.code:
type UnwrapPromise<T> = T extends Promise<infer V> ? V : T;
type Wrapped = UnwrapPromise<Promise<number[]>>;
questions.explanation: `Promise<number[]>` matches `Promise<infer V>` with V = `number[]`. The conditional is true — result is V = `number[]`. Note: this unwraps ONE layer. For nested Promises use a recursive variant like `Awaited<T>`.

questions.prompt: Key remapping with `as` in a mapped type allows:
choices: Renaming the output keys using a template literal or conditional expression at the type level
questions.explanation: `[K in keyof T as NewKey]` remaps the key. The classic use: `` `get${Capitalize<string & K>}` `` turns `{ name: string }` into `{ getName: () => string }`. Zero runtime impact — purely a type-level rename.

questions.prompt: Which TypeScript utility types are implemented as mapped types?
questions.explanation: `Partial`, `Readonly`, and `Required` are all mapped types — they iterate over `keyof T` and transform each property. `ReturnType` is a conditional type with `infer`, not a mapped type.

questions.prompt: The `-?` modifier in `{ [K in keyof T]-?: T[K] }` means:
choices: Remove the optional modifier — make all properties required
questions.explanation: Mapped type modifiers: `+?` adds optional (default), `-?` removes optional (making required). Likewise `+readonly` / `-readonly`. `Required<T>` uses `-?` to strip all optional markers.

questions.prompt: What happens when conditional types distribute over `never`?
questions.code:
type OnlyString<T> = T extends string ? T : never;
type R = OnlyString<never>;
questions.explanation: When T = `never` (the empty union), distributing over it produces `never` — there are no members to iterate over. `OnlyString<never> = never`. This is often surprising but mathematically consistent.

questions.prompt: What does `string & K` do inside `Capitalize<string & K>` in a mapped type?
choices: Constrains K to be a string so Capitalize can accept it — K is keyof T which may be string | symbol | number
Creates a union of string and K
Checks that K extends string at runtime
questions.explanation: `keyof T` produces `string | number | symbol`. `Capitalize` only accepts `string`. The intersection `string & K` narrows to only the string keys — filtering out number and symbol keys from the remapping.

questions.prompt: Why might a recursive type like `DeepPartial<T>` hit TypeScript's depth limit?
choices: TypeScript doesn't support recursive types at all
Deeply nested objects cause TypeScript to evaluate the type recursively — very deep nesting can hit internal compiler limits
DeepPartial is not available until TypeScript 5.5
Recursive types require explicit depth parameters
questions.explanation: TypeScript evaluates recursive types lazily but has internal depth limits (typically ~100 levels). For most real-world data shapes this is fine. For extremely deep nesting or circular types, you may need to use a different approach.

questions.prompt: What type is `FnReturn`?
questions.code:
type ExtractReturn<T> = T extends (...args: any[]) => infer R ? R : never;
type FnReturn = ExtractReturn<(x: number) => string[]>;
questions.explanation: `(x: number) => string[]` matches `(...args: any[]) => infer R` with R = `string[]`. The function return type is captured as string[].

questions.prompt: Conditional type distributivity can be prevented by wrapping T in a tuple:
choices: True — `[T] extends [U] ? A : B` does NOT distribute over union T
questions.explanation: To prevent distribution: wrap in brackets. `[T] extends [string] ? T : never` with T = `string | number` gives `string | number` (the full union, not filtered). This is the escape hatch when distribution would give undesired results.

questions.prompt: Which are valid positions for `infer` in a conditional type?
choices: T extends (infer U)[]    — inside an array type pattern
T extends Promise<infer U>  — inside a generic type parameter
T extends infer U         — directly as extends target
type Alias = infer U      — standalone outside conditional
questions.explanation: Options A, B, and C are valid — `infer` can appear in the `extends` clause of a conditional at any point where a type could appear. Option D is invalid — `infer` only has meaning inside a `T extends ... ?` conditional and cannot be used standalone.

questions.prompt: Template literal types can be used to:
choices: Create union types of string patterns, generate property name unions, and constrain string shapes at compile time
Only work with single string values, not unions
questions.explanation: Template literals at the type level can construct new string literal unions from existing ones (e.g., `` `get${Capitalize<EventName>}` ``), constrain function parameters to specific patterns, and in combination with mapped types, generate entire interfaces dynamically.

questions.prompt: What is the practical value of writing `type JSONValue = string | number | boolean | null | JSONValue[] | { [k: string]: JSONValue }`?
choices: It speeds up JSON.parse at runtime
It creates a self-referencing type that describes any valid JSON structure — TypeScript validates deeply nested JSON-compatible values without manual depth limits
It prevents null from appearing in JSON
questions.explanation: Recursive types let TypeScript validate arbitrarily nested structures. Any value typed as `JSONValue` must be one of those forms — including objects with `JSONValue` values and arrays of `JSONValue`. This catches values like `undefined` or `Date` objects that aren't valid JSON.

flashcards.back: `T extends U ? A : B` — if T is assignable to U, use type A; otherwise type B. Runs at compile time. Zero JavaScript output.
When T is a union, `T extends U ? A : B` applies to EACH member separately and unions the results. `string | number extends string ? T : never` → `string | never` = `string`.
Captures part of a matched type inside a conditional extends clause. `T extends Array<infer U>` captures the element type as U. Only valid INSIDE a conditional type extends expression.
`{ [K in keyof T]: NewType }` — iterate over all keys of T, produce a transformed type. Add `?` for optional, `readonly` for immutable, `as Rename` for key remapping.
`` `prefix_${T}` `` constructs new string literal unions. Distributes over union T. Combined with Capitalize/Lowercase/Uppercase/Uncapitalize for casing transforms.
Pass the default branch to `(x: never) => never`. Compiles only if all union cases are handled. Adding a new case without updating the switch = compile error immediately.
`[T] extends [U] ? A : B` wraps T in a tuple, preventing distributivity. Use when you want the conditional to test T as a whole union, not member by member.
`-?` in a mapped type REMOVES the optional modifier. Example: `Required<T>` = `{ [K in keyof T]-?: T[K] }`. Use `+?` to add optional, `-readonly` to remove readonly.

project.brief: Build a type-level 'Event System' using advanced types. You have an event map: `{ click: MouseEvent; keydown: KeyboardEvent; resize: UIEvent }`. Task: 1) Write a generic `EventHandler<Map, K extends keyof Map>` type that extracts the correct event type for a given key. 2) Write a `AllHandlers<Map>` mapped type that produces `{ onClick: (e: MouseEvent) => void; onKeydown: (e: KeyboardEvent) => void; onResize: (e: UIEvent) => void }` — with 'on' prefix and Capitalize applied to key names. 3) Write an `ExtractEventType<Map, K>` using `infer` in a conditional type to extract event types from functions stored in the map. 4) If you add a new event `hover: PointerEvent` to the map, which types update automatically and which require manual changes?

checklist.text: EventHandler<Map, K> uses indexed access T[K] to get event type
AllHandlers<Map> uses mapped type with template literal key remapping
ExtractEventType uses infer in a conditional type to capture event type from a handler function type
Explains that mapped types (AllHandlers) auto-update; manually written Record-style types need updating

hints: EventHandler: `type EventHandler<Map, K extends keyof Map> = (e: Map[K]) => void` — indexed access for event type.
AllHandlers key remap: `[K in keyof Map as `on${Capitalize<string & K>}`]: (e: Map[K]) => void`.
ExtractEventType: `T extends (e: infer E) => void ? E : never` — infer the event param type.
Mapped types regenerate for every key — adding 'hover' to Map immediately adds 'onHover' to AllHandlers.
